intro: Slack and Plain diverge first on pricing model: Slack uses freemium pricing with a free tier, Plain uses seat pricing with no free tier. Everything else flows from that commercial choice. Slack is built for tech-forward companies needing a strong chat platform with a rich app ecosystem and is not a fit for microsoft-standardized orgs or teams prioritizing suite-bundled collaboration. Plain is built for apps and is not a fit for products without customer support. On the scoring side, Slack rates 5/5 for developer experience and 5/5 for transparency; Plain rates 4/5 and 4/5 respectively. The honest trade-off: with Slack you accept that pricing feels punitive at scale, while Plain comes with the reality that products without customer support. Neither side is free. Slack's headline strength in the data is that default chat for modern knowledge work. Plain's headline strength is that in-app customer support chat. Read each side's best-for and not-for fields carefully before you pick; the shape of your team and workflow matters more than any single score.
quick_take: Slack is for tech-forward companies needing a strong chat platform with a rich app ecosystem; Plain is for apps; decide based on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Slack if you are tech-forward companies needing a strong chat platform with a rich app ecosystem and a freemium pricing model fits how you buy.
verdict_b: Choose Plain if you are apps and a seat pricing model fits how you buy.

faq.a: Slack uses a freemium model, while Plain uses a seat model; Slack has a free tier and Plain does not. We don't have per-seat numbers in this dataset, so 'cheaper' depends on volume. Price both on your actual usage before deciding.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Slack to Plain?
faq.a: Slack has medium lock-in (data exportable, ecosystem lock); Plain has medium lock-in (seat-based, data exportable via api). Migration effort scales with how much state and automation you've built in Slack; plan for data export, config rebuild, and a parallel-run period before cutting over.
In our data, Slack scores 5/5 for developer experience versus 4/5 for Plain. That said, DX is subjective; the Plain score may still be acceptable if its feature fit is stronger for your use case.

faq.q: Is Plain a good alternative to Slack?
faq.a: Plain is built for apps and explicitly not for products without customer support, so it's a fit only if your workflow matches its stated audience. If you were using Slack for tech-forward companies needing a strong chat platform with a rich app ecosystem, the switch is reasonable; outside that scope, look elsewhere.
